text:
PET-CT WHOLE BODY WITH FDG

PATIENT: Raymond Kowalski
INDICATION: Initial staging of newly diagnosed sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma. Known synchronous bilobar hepatic metastases on prior CT. Evaluate for extent of FDG-avid disease and presence of occult metastatic sites prior to initiation of systemic chemotherapy.

COMPARISON: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (staging CT, performed several days prior).

TECHNIQUE: Whole-body PET-CT was performed following intravenous administration of 11.2 mCi of F-18 fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG). The patient fasted for a minimum of 6 hours prior to injection. Pre-injection serum glucose was 108 mg/dL. Images were acquired from the vertex of the skull through the proximal thighs at approximately 90 minutes post-injection. Low-dose CT was performed for attenuation correction and anatomic localization. Images were reviewed in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes on fused PET-CT, PET-only, and CT-only display formats. SUV values are standardized uptake values corrected for body weight.

---

FINDINGS:

HEAD AND NECK:
No pathologically FDG-avid cervical, supraclavicular, or submandibular lymphadenopathy. No FDG-avid intracranial lesions, though PET is of limited sensitivity for evaluation of brain parenchyma. No FDG-avid osseous lesions involving the calvarium or cervical spine. Mild symmetric FDG uptake within the bilateral parotid and submandibular glands, within physiologic limits. No focal mucosal FDG uptake to suggest an occult head and neck primary or metastasis.

CHEST:
Lungs: No FDG-avid pulmonary nodules or parenchymal lesions. The lungs appear clear on low-dose CT attenuation correction images; no discrete nodules are identified. There is no pleural effusion. No FDG-avid pleural implants.

Mediastinum and Hila: Mild, symmetric, and diffuse FDG uptake is seen in the mediastinal blood pool (mean SUV approximately 2.1), within expected physiologic range. No FDG-avid mediastinal or hilar lymph nodes. A few small mediastinal lymph nodes are noted on CT (short axis up to 7 mm), none with abnormal FDG uptake. No FDG-avid pericardial or myocardial abnormality beyond normal physiologic cardiac uptake.

Chest wall and axillae: No FDG-avid axillary lymphadenopathy. No FDG-avid osseous lesions involving the ribs, sternum, clavicles, or thoracic spine.

ABDOMEN:
Liver: Four hepatic lesions with pathologically elevated FDG uptake are identified, corresponding to lesions previously characterized on CT:
  - Segment VI: A heterogeneously FDG-avid mass measuring approximately 3.8 cm in greatest dimension on the concurrent low-dose CT. SUVmax 12.4. The lesion demonstrates intense peripheral and mildly heterogeneous central uptake, with a photopenic center consistent with central hypoxia or necrosis, a pattern commonly seen in larger hepatic metastases from colorectal primaries.
  - Segment II: A mildly lobulated FDG-avid focus measuring approximately 1.9 cm. SUVmax 9.6. Uptake is fairly homogeneous.
  - Segment IV: An FDG-avid lesion measuring approximately 2.2 cm. SUVmax 10.8. Uptake is moderately intense and relatively homogeneous.
  - Segment VII: A small FDG-avid focus measuring approximately 1.1 cm, near the hepatic dome. SUVmax 8.7. The lesion is discrete and well-demarcated from the adjacent normal hepatic parenchyma, which demonstrates low background uptake (mean hepatic SUV 2.6).
  No additional FDG-avid hepatic lesions beyond the four identified above. No FDG-avid perihepatic lymphadenopathy. Normal physiologic FDG uptake is seen in the hepatic parenchyma, gallbladder wall (physiologic), and biliary system.

Spleen: Normal size and physiologic FDG uptake. No focal splenic lesions.

Pancreas: Normal physiologic uptake throughout the pancreatic body and tail. No FDG-avid pancreatic mass or peripancreatic adenopathy.

Kidneys and Adrenal Glands: Physiologic FDG excretion is noted within the bilateral renal collecting systems and ureters. No focal FDG-avid renal parenchymal lesions. Bilateral adrenal glands demonstrate normal morphology and physiologic FDG uptake. No FDG-avid adrenal lesions to suggest metastatic involvement.

Stomach and Small Bowel: Physiologic heterogeneous FDG activity is noted in the gastric wall and scattered loops of small bowel, within expected limits. No focal pathologically elevated bowel uptake beyond the primary lesion described below.

Colon and Rectosigmoid: An intensely FDG-avid mass is identified in the sigmoid colon, corresponding to the known primary tumor. The lesion spans approximately 4 to 5 cm in length along the sigmoid colon wall, with transmural and pericolonic extension evident on the CT component. SUVmax 14.2. The uptake is markedly elevated, considerably above the background colonic mucosal activity. Pericolic and mesenteric fat stranding is visible on CT at this level. Several pericolonic lymph nodes in the adjacent mesentery demonstrate mildly elevated FDG uptake, the largest measuring approximately 1.4 cm on CT with an SUVmax of 4.8, compatible with nodal metastatic involvement. No additional FDG-avid colonic lesions suggesting synchronous primary tumors.

Peritoneum and Retroperitoneum: No FDG-avid peritoneal implants. No ascites. No FDG-avid retroperitoneal or mesenteric adenopathy beyond the pericolic nodal disease described. The aorta and inferior vena cava appear normal in caliber.

PELVIS:
Bladder: Physiologic FDG excretion in the urinary bladder. No focal bladder wall uptake.

Rectum: No FDG-avid rectal lesion. The rectum appears normal in caliber with physiologic mucosal uptake.

Pelvic lymph nodes: No FDG-avid inguinal, iliac, or obturator lymphadenopathy.

Pelvic organs: No FDG-avid pelvic floor lesions. Prostate demonstrates mild symmetric physiologic uptake, unremarkable.

MUSCULOSKELETAL:
No FDG-avid osseous lesions involving the axial or appendicular skeleton to suggest osseous metastatic disease. The visualized osseous structures demonstrate normal physiologic bone marrow uptake. No suspicious lytic or sclerotic lesions on the low-dose CT component involving the spine, pelvis, or proximal femora. Mild degenerative changes are noted in the lumbar spine, within expected range for patient age.

SKIN AND SOFT TISSUES:
No FDG-avid subcutaneous nodules or soft tissue masses. No evidence of cutaneous metastases.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Intensely FDG-avid primary sigmoid colon mass (SUVmax 14.2), with associated FDG-avid pericolonic mesenteric lymphadenopathy, consistent with the known primary adenocarcinoma and nodal metastatic involvement as characterized on recent CT.

2. Four FDG-avid hepatic metastases in bilobar distribution (segments II, IV, VI, and VII; SUVmax range 8.7-12.4), concordant with the lesions identified on the recent contrast-enhanced CT. No additional occult hepatic foci of FDG-avid disease are identified beyond these four known lesions.

3. No FDG-avid pulmonary nodules. The lungs appear clear. There is no evidence of thoracic nodal, pleural, or parenchymal metastatic disease at this time.

4. No FDG-avid peritoneal implants to suggest peritoneal carcinomatosis.

5. No FDG-avid osseous, adrenal, or other extrahepatic sites of metastatic disease identified.

6. Overall, the PET-CT findings are concordant with and confirm the extent of disease characterized on the recent staging CT. No additional sites of occult metastatic disease are revealed beyond the known hepatic and locoregional involvement. The hepatic metastatic burden remains the dominant site of distant disease.

Interpreting Radiologist: [Attending Radiologist, Nuclear Medicine/Radiology]
Residency-trained Nuclear Medicine Physician / Diagnostic Radiology
[Institution - Nuclear Medicine Division]
